ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for battered fish fillet as a prepared food product; SNOMED CT coverage for prepared dishes is limited.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard battered fish fillet piece weighs approximately 100–120 g and is typically served as a main protein component in a Western-style meal or fish-and-chips serving.

nutrient_source.title: Battered Fish Fillet — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for battered fish fillet (cr_id F03020002). All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this record with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for battered fish fillet in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 65 (medium) based on analogous deep-fried battered foods with wheat-flour coatings; the batter contributes refined carbohydrates (13.26 g/100g) with low fibre (0.71 g/100g), consistent with medium-GI fried battered products. Glycemic load per 110 g serving = (65 × 13.26 × 1.10) / 100 ≈ 9.4.

hypertension.key_factor: Very high sodium content at 693 mg per 100 g, exceeding the 600 mg/100 g threshold for hypertension concern
hypertension.guidance: Individuals managing hypertension should avoid battered fish fillet due to its very high sodium content of 693 mg/100 g; if consumed, strict portion control (no more than 50 g) is essential and should be paired with potassium-rich vegetables to partially offset sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Refined wheat-flour batter contributes 13.26 g carbohydrate per 100 g with only 0.71 g dietary fibre, yielding a medium GI of approximately 65 and elevated glycaemic load; high fat from deep-frying further impairs post-meal insulin sensitivity
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should limit or avoid battered fish fillet because the refined batter carbohydrates and deep-frying fat combine to elevate post-meal blood glucose and worsen insulin resistance; grilled or steamed white fish is a strongly preferred alternative.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High total fat (17.81 g/100 g) including saturated fat (~2.8 g/100 g) and estimated trans fat (~0.3 g/100 g) from deep-frying, combined with very high sodium (693 mg/100 g), raises risk for dyslipidaemia, arterial stiffness, and elevated blood pressure
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid battered fish fillet due to its combination of high sodium, saturated fat, and trans fat from deep-frying, all of which adversely affect lipid profiles and vascular health; baked or grilled fish without batter is the recommended alternative.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) and trans fat (~0.3 g/100 g estimated), both of which amplify inflammatory cytokine activity relevant to rheumatoid arthritis
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid deep-fried battered fish as AGEs and trans fat from frying directly worsen joint inflammation; steamed or baked fish retains anti-inflammatory omega-3 benefits without the pro-inflammatory compounds.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Battered fish fillet is a Western-style dish consisting of a white fish fillet (typically cod, haddock, or pollock) coated in a seasoned wheat-flour batter and deep-fried in oil until the exterior is golden and crispy while the interior remains moist and flaky. The batter typically contains wheat flour, water or beer, salt, and leavening agents, which puff up during frying to create a light, crunchy crust. Per 100 g, the dish provides 259 kcal, 11.48 g protein, 17.81 g fat, and 13.26 g carbohydrate, with a notably high sodium content of 693 mg. The dietary fibre content is low at 0.71 g/100 g. The deep-frying process adds substantial fat and generates trans fats and advanced glycation end-products (AGEs), making this dish a high-concern food for individuals managing hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, cardiovascular disease, or rheumatoid arthritis. The glycaemic index is estimated at approximately 65 (medium) based on the refined batter carbohydrate profile. In Singapore and across Western food culture, battered fish fillet is commonly served as part of a fish-and-chips meal, in fast-food settings, or as a hawker-centre Western food stall item. Healthier alternatives include grilled, steamed, or baked fish preparations that preserve the protein and omega-3 content of white fish without the added fat, sodium, and AGEs from deep-frying.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F03020002) with a stated confidence of 0.95. The food is a well-defined Western-style prepared dish with a canonical English name. GI is an analogous estimate (medium confidence for GI sub-field only) as no direct measurement exists in the University of Sydney GI Database for this exact preparation.
notes: sugar_g is confirmed zero per HPB source — batter formulation contains no added sugar and the fish itself contributes negligible sugar; confirmed zero per HPB Foodo database (cr_id F03020002). The following per_100g fields are set to -1 because detailed fatty acid breakdown, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this specific battered fish fillet preparation are not individually reported in the HPB Foodo database entry for F03020002 as of 2026, and no supplementary approved source (USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy, USDA Foundation Foods) reports these values for an exact match to this preparation: monounsaturated_fat_g — detailed fatty acid fractions not reported by HPB for this entry; polyunsaturated_fat_g — detailed fatty acid fractions not reported by HPB for this entry; potassium_mg — micronutrient not reported by HPB for this entry; calcium_mg — micronutrient not reported by HPB for this entry; iron_mg — micronutrient not reported by HPB for this entry; cholesterol_mg — not reported by HPB for this entry; vitamin_c_mg — not reported by HPB for this entry; vitamin_a_mcg_rae — not reported by HPB for this entry; vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg — not reported by HPB for this entry; vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg — not reported by HPB for this entry; vitamin_b3_niacin_mg — not reported by HPB for this entry; vitamin_b6_mg — not reported by HPB for this entry; vitamin_b12_mcg — not reported by HPB for this entry; folate_mcg_dfe — not reported by HPB for this entry; vitamin_d_mcg — not reported by HPB for this entry; vitamin_e_mg — not reported by HPB for this entry; phosphorus_mg — not reported by HPB for this entry; magnesium_mg — not reported by HPB for this entry; zinc_mg — not reported by HPB for this entry; selenium_mcg — not reported by HPB for this entry; water_g — moisture data not reported by HPB for this entry. trans_fat_g is estimated at 0.3 g/100 g based on typical deep-frying conditions for wheat-flour battered fish; this is an estimate and not a direct HPB measurement. saturated_fat_g of 2.8 g/100 g is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy analogous entry for battered/breaded fish fillet as HPB does not report saturated fat separately for this item. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate based on deep-fried battered wheat-flour coated foods from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for battered fish fillet was found. Halal status is assumed based on typical commercial preparation with permissible fish and vegetable oil; kosher status is not confirmed. This food is not a Singaporean dish and therefore multilingual naming requirements for Chinese, Malay, Tamil, and Hindi do not apply; cuisine is Western.
